subject: Mathematics
question_text: Solve the following linear programming problem graphically: Maximise Z = 4x + y subject to the constraints: x + y {tex}\le{/tex} 50 3x + y {tex}\le{/tex} 90 x {tex}\ge{/tex} 0, y {tex}\ge{/tex} 0
question_html:
Solve the following linear programming problem graphically:
Maximise Z = 4x + y subject to the constraints: 
x + y {tex}\le{/tex} 50
3x + y {tex}\le{/tex} 90
x {tex}\ge{/tex} 0, y {tex}\ge{/tex} 0
solution_text: To Maximize Z = 4x + y ......(i) subject to the constraints: x + y {tex}\le{/tex} 50 .....(ii) 3x + y {tex}\le{/tex} 90 ......(iii) x {tex}\ge{/tex} 0, y {tex}\ge{/tex} 0 .....(iv) The shaded region in a figure is the feasible region determined by the system of constraints (ii) to (iv). We observe that the feasible region OABC is bounded. So, we now use Corner Point Method to determine the maximum value of Z. The coordinates of the corner points O, A, B and C are (0, 0), (30, 0), (20, 30) and (0, 50) respectively. Now we evaluate Z at each corner point. Corner Point Corresponding value of Z (0, 0) 0 (30, 0) 120 (20, 30) 110 (0, 50) 50 Hence, maximum value of Z is 120 at the point (30, 0).
solution_html:
To Maximize Z = 4x + y ......(i)
subject to the constraints:
x + y {tex}\le{/tex} 50  .....(ii)
3x + y {tex}\le{/tex} 90 ......(iii)
x {tex}\ge{/tex} 0, y {tex}\ge{/tex} 0 .....(iv)
The shaded region in a figure is the feasible region determined by the system of constraints (ii) to (iv). We observe that the feasible region OABC is bounded. So, we now use Corner Point Method to determine the maximum value of Z.
The coordinates of the corner points O, A, B and C are (0, 0), (30, 0), (20, 30) and (0, 50) respectively. Now we evaluate Z at each corner point.

Corner Point Corresponding value of Z
(0, 0) 0
(30, 0) 120
(20, 30) 110
(0, 50) 50


Hence, maximum value of Z is 120 at the point (30, 0).

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Solve the following linear programming problem graphically: Minimise Z = 200x + 500y subject to the constraints: {tex}x + 2 y \geq 10{/tex} {tex}3 x + 4 y \leq 24{/tex} {tex}x \geq 0 , y \geq 0{/tex}
question_html:
Solve the following linear programming problem graphically:
Minimise Z = 200x + 500y subject to the constraints:
{tex}x + 2 y \geq 10{/tex}
{tex}3 x + 4 y \leq 24{/tex}
{tex}x \geq 0 , y \geq 0{/tex}
solution_text: According to the question, the linear constraints {tex}x + 2 y \geq 10{/tex} {tex}3 x + 4 y \leq 24{/tex} and {tex}x \geq 0 , y \geq 0{/tex} and objective function is Min {tex}(Z) = 200x + 500y.{/tex} Reducing the above inequations into equations {tex}x + 2y = 10{/tex} ...(i) {tex}3x + 4y = 24{/tex} ...(ii) {tex}x = 0, y = 0{/tex} ...(iii) Equations Point of Intersection (i) and (ii) x = 4 and y = 3 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Point is {tex} (4, 3){/tex} (i) and (iii) when x = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = 5 when y = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 10 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Points are {tex}(0, 5), (10, 0){/tex} (ii) and (iii) when x = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = 6 when y = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 8 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Points are {tex}(0, 6) and (8, 0){/tex} For {tex}x + 2 y \geq 10{/tex} , putting x = 0 and y = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow 0 + 0 \geq 10 \Rightarrow 0 \geq 10{/tex} i.e., Not true {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} The shaded region will be away from origin. Likewise, for {tex}3 x + 4 y \leq 24{/tex} , putting x = 0 and y = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow 0 + 0 \leq 24 \Rightarrow 0 \leq 24{/tex} i.e. true {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} the shaded region will be toward the origin Also, we have, {tex}x \geq 0{/tex} and {tex}y \geq 0{/tex} , indicates that the shaded part will exist in first quadrant only. Here, feasible region or bounded region will be {tex}ABCA{/tex} , having corner points as {tex}A(0,6) B(4, 3)\ and\ C(0, 5){/tex} . For optimal point substituting the value of all-corner points in {tex}Z = 200x + 500y{/tex} Corner points Z {tex}A (0, 6){/tex} {tex}3000{/tex} {tex}B (4, 3){/tex} {tex}2300{/tex} {tex}\to{/tex} Minimum {tex}C (0, 5){/tex} {tex}2500{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} The minimum value of ‘Z’ is 2300, exist at B (4, 3). Here point B is known as optimal point and min(Z) as optimal solution.
solution_html:
According to the question, the linear constraints
{tex}x + 2 y \geq 10{/tex}
{tex}3 x + 4 y \leq 24{/tex}
and {tex}x \geq 0 , y \geq 0{/tex}
and objective function is Min {tex}(Z) = 200x + 500y.{/tex}
Reducing the above inequations into equations
{tex}x + 2y = 10{/tex} ...(i)
{tex}3x + 4y = 24{/tex} ...(ii)
{tex}x = 0, y = 0{/tex} ...(iii)

Equations Point of Intersection
(i) and (ii) x = 4 and y = 3
  {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Point is {tex} (4, 3){/tex}
(i) and (iii) when x = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = 5
  when y = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 10
  {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Points are {tex}(0, 5), (10, 0){/tex}
(ii) and (iii) when x = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = 6
  when y = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 8
  {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Points are {tex}(0, 6) and (8, 0){/tex}


For {tex}x + 2 y \geq 10{/tex}, putting x = 0 and y = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow 0 + 0 \geq 10 \Rightarrow 0 \geq 10{/tex} i.e., Not true
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} The shaded region will be away from origin.
Likewise, for {tex}3 x + 4 y \leq 24{/tex}, putting x = 0 and y = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow 0 + 0 \leq 24 \Rightarrow 0 \leq 24{/tex} i.e. true
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} the shaded region will be toward the origin
Also, we have, {tex}x \geq 0{/tex} and {tex}y \geq 0{/tex}, indicates that the shaded part will exist in first quadrant only. Here, feasible region or bounded region will be {tex}ABCA{/tex}, having corner points as {tex}A(0,6) B(4, 3)\ and\ C(0, 5){/tex}. For optimal point substituting the value of all-corner points in {tex}Z = 200x + 500y{/tex}

Corner points Z
{tex}A (0, 6){/tex} {tex}3000{/tex}
{tex}B (4, 3){/tex} {tex}2300{/tex} {tex}\to{/tex} Minimum
{tex}C (0, 5){/tex} {tex}2500{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} The minimum value of ‘Z’ is 2300, exist at B (4, 3). Here point B is known as optimal point and min(Z) as optimal solution.

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Solve the following problem graphically: Minimise or Maximise Z = 3x + 9y subject to the constraints: {tex}x + 3 y \leq 60{/tex} {tex}x + y \geq 10{/tex} {tex}x \leq y{/tex} {tex}x \geq 0 , y \geq 0{/tex}
question_html:
Solve the following problem graphically:
Minimise or Maximise Z = 3x + 9y subject to the constraints:
{tex}x + 3 y \leq 60{/tex}
{tex}x + y \geq 10{/tex}
{tex}x \leq y{/tex}
{tex}x \geq 0 , y \geq 0{/tex}
solution_text: The linear inequations or constraints {tex}x + 3 y \leq 60{/tex} {tex}x + y \geq 10{/tex} {tex}x \leq y{/tex} {tex}x \geq 0 , y \geq 0{/tex} and objective functions is max or min {tex}(Z) = 3x + 9y{/tex} Reducing the above inequations into equations and finding their point of intersection i.e., {tex}x + 3y = 60{/tex} ... (i) {tex}x + y = 10{/tex} ... (ii) {tex}x = y{/tex} ... (iii) {tex}x = 0, y = 0 {/tex} ... (iv) Equations Point of Intersection (i) and (ii) {tex}x = -15, y = 25{/tex} Point is {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}(-15, 25){/tex} (i) and (iii) x = 15 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = 15 Point is {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}(15, 15){/tex} (ii) and (iii) {tex}x = 5, y = 5{/tex} Point is {tex}(5, 5){/tex} (i) and (iv) when x = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = 20, Point is {tex} (0, 20){/tex} when y = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 60, Point is {tex}(60, 0){/tex} (ii) and (iv) when x = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = 10, Point is {tex}(0, 10){/tex} when y = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 10, Point is {tex}(10, 0){/tex} Now for feasible region, For {tex}x + 3 y \leq 60{/tex} , putting x = 0 and y = 0, we have {tex}0 + 0 \geq 10{/tex} i.e., Not true {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} The shaded region will be away from origin. Also, we have {tex}x \geq 0{/tex} , and {tex}y \geq 0{/tex} indicates that the shaded part will exist in first quadrant only. Here feasible region will be ABCDA, having corner points as {tex}A(0, 20), B(15, 15), C(5, 5)\ and\ D(0, 10){/tex} . For optimal point substituting the value of all corner points in objective function {tex}Z = 3x + 9y{/tex} Corner points Z {tex}A (0, 20){/tex} {tex}180{/tex} Maximum {tex}B (15, 15){/tex} {tex}180{/tex} {tex}C (5, 5){/tex} {tex}60{/tex} Minimum {tex}D (0, 10){/tex} {tex}90{/tex} So that the minimum value of Z is 60 at C (5, 5) of the feasible region and the maximum value at A (0, 20) and B(15, 15) is Z = 180.
solution_html:
The linear inequations or constraints 
{tex}x + 3 y \leq 60{/tex}
{tex}x + y \geq 10{/tex}
{tex}x \leq y{/tex}
{tex}x \geq 0 , y \geq 0{/tex} and objective functions is max or min {tex}(Z) = 3x + 9y{/tex}
Reducing the above inequations into equations and finding their point of intersection i.e.,
{tex}x + 3y = 60{/tex} ... (i)
{tex}x + y = 10{/tex} ... (ii)
{tex}x = y{/tex} ... (iii)
{tex}x = 0, y = 0 {/tex}... (iv)

Equations Point of Intersection
(i) and (ii) {tex}x = -15, y = 25{/tex}
  Point is {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}(-15, 25){/tex}
(i) and (iii) x = 15 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = 15
  Point is {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}(15, 15){/tex}
(ii) and (iii) {tex}x = 5, y = 5{/tex}
  Point is {tex}(5, 5){/tex}
(i) and (iv) when x = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = 20,
  Point is{tex} (0, 20){/tex}
  when y = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 60,
  Point is {tex}(60, 0){/tex}
(ii) and (iv) when x = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = 10,
  Point is {tex}(0, 10){/tex}
  when y = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 10,
  Point is {tex}(10, 0){/tex}

Now for feasible region, 
For {tex}x + 3 y \leq 60{/tex}, putting x = 0 and y = 0, we have
{tex}0 + 0 \geq 10{/tex} i.e., Not true
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} The shaded region will be away from origin.
Also, we have {tex}x \geq 0{/tex}, and {tex}y \geq 0{/tex} indicates that the shaded part will exist in first quadrant only. Here feasible region will be ABCDA, having corner points as {tex}A(0, 20), B(15, 15), C(5, 5)\ and\ D(0, 10){/tex}.
For optimal point substituting the value of all corner points in objective function {tex}Z = 3x + 9y{/tex}

Corner points Z
{tex}A (0, 20){/tex} {tex}180{/tex} Maximum
{tex}B (15, 15){/tex} {tex}180{/tex}
{tex}C (5, 5){/tex} {tex}60{/tex} Minimum
{tex}D (0, 10){/tex} {tex}90{/tex}

So that the minimum value of Z is 60 at C (5, 5) of the feasible region and the maximum value at A (0, 20) and B(15, 15) is Z = 180.

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Determine graphically the minimum value of the objective function Z = -50x + 20y subject to the constraints: 2x - y {tex}\geq{/tex} - 5 3x + y {tex}\geq{/tex} 3 2x - 3y {tex}\leq{/tex} 12 x {tex}\geq{/tex} 0, y {tex}\geq{/tex} 0
question_html:
Determine graphically the minimum value of the objective function Z = -50x + 20y subject to the constraints:
2x - y {tex}\geq{/tex} - 5
3x + y {tex}\geq{/tex} 3
2x - 3y {tex}\leq{/tex} 12
x {tex}\geq{/tex} 0, y {tex}\geq{/tex} 0
solution_text: 2x - y {tex}\geq{/tex} - 5 3x + y {tex}\geq{/tex} 3 2x - 3y {tex}\leq{/tex} 12 x {tex}\geq{/tex} 0, y {tex}\geq{/tex} 0 The feasible region of the system of inequations given in constraints is shown in a figure. We observe that the feasible region is unbounded. The values of the objective function Z at the comer points are given in the following table: Corner point (x, y) Value of the objective function Z = -50x + 20y (0,5) Z = - 50 {tex}\times{/tex} 0 + 20 {tex}\times{/tex} 5 = 100 (0,3) Z = - 50 {tex}\times{/tex} 0 + 20 {tex}\times{/tex} 3 = 60 (1,0) Z = - 50 {tex}\times{/tex} 1 + 20 {tex}\times{/tex} 0 = - 50 (6,0) Z = -50 {tex}\times{/tex} 6 + 20 {tex}\times{/tex} 0 = - 300 Clearly, - 300 is the smallest value of Z at the corner point (6, 0). Since the feasible region is unbounded, therefore, to check whether - 300 is the minimum value of Z, we draw the line - 300 = -50x + 20y and check whether the open half plane -50 x + 20y < -300 has points in common with the feasible region or not. From Fig., we find that the open half plane represented by - 50 x + 20y < - 300 has points in common with the feasible region. Therefore, Z = - 50x + 20y has no minimum value subject to the given constraints.
solution_html:
2x - y {tex}\geq{/tex} - 5
3x + y {tex}\geq{/tex} 3
2x - 3y {tex}\leq{/tex} 12
x {tex}\geq{/tex} 0, y {tex}\geq{/tex} 0
The feasible region of the system of inequations given in constraints is shown in a figure. We observe that the feasible region is unbounded.

The values of the objective function Z at the comer points are given in the following table:

Corner point (x, y) Value of the objective function Z = -50x + 20y
(0,5) Z = - 50 {tex}\times{/tex} 0 + 20 {tex}\times{/tex} 5 = 100
(0,3) Z = - 50 {tex}\times{/tex} 0 + 20 {tex}\times{/tex} 3 = 60
(1,0) Z = - 50 {tex}\times{/tex} 1 + 20 {tex}\times{/tex} 0 = - 50
(6,0) Z = -50 {tex}\times{/tex} 6 + 20 {tex}\times{/tex} 0 = - 300

Clearly, - 300 is the smallest value of Z at the corner point (6, 0). Since the feasible region is unbounded, therefore, to check whether - 300 is the minimum value of Z, we draw the line - 300 = -50x + 20y and check whether the open half plane -50 x + 20y < -300 has points in common with the feasible region or not. From Fig., we find that the open half plane represented by - 50 x + 20y < - 300 has points in common with the feasible region. Therefore, Z = - 50x + 20y has no minimum value subject to the given constraints.

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Minimize Z = 3x + 2y subject to the constraints: {tex}x + y \geq 8{/tex} {tex}3 x + 5 y \leq 15{/tex} {tex}x \geq 0 , \ y \geq 0{/tex}
question_html:
Minimize Z = 3x + 2y subject to the constraints:
{tex}x + y \geq 8{/tex}
{tex}3 x + 5 y \leq 15{/tex}
{tex}x \geq 0 , \ y \geq 0{/tex}
solution_text: Linear constraints {tex}x + y \geq 8{/tex} {tex}3 x + 5 y \leq 15{/tex} {tex}x \geq 0 , y \geq 0{/tex} Objective function is min {tex} (Z) = 3x + 2y{/tex} Reducing the all inequations into equations and finding their point of intersections, i.e., {tex}x + y = 8{/tex} ... (i) {tex}3x + 5y = 15{/tex} ... (ii) {tex}x = 0, y = 0{/tex} ... (iii) Equations Point of Intersection (i) and (ii) {tex}x = \frac { 25 } { 2 } \text { and } y = \frac { - 9 } { 2 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \left( \frac { 25 } { 2 } , \frac { - 9 } { 2 } \right){/tex} (i) and (iii) when x = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = 8 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (0, 8) when y = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 8 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (8, 0) (ii) and (iii) when x = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = 3 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (0, 3) when y = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 5 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (5, 0) For feasible region. For {tex}x + y \geq 8{/tex} let {tex}x = 0, y = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 0 \geq 8{/tex} i.e., Not true {tex}\Rightarrow {/tex} The shaded region will be away from origin Again, for {tex}3 x + 5 y \leq 15{/tex} , let {tex}x = 0, y = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 0 \leq 15{/tex} i.e. true We have, no negative restriction, {tex}x \geq 0, y \geq 0{/tex} indicates that the shaded region will exist in first quadrant only. The problem will not have any feasible region. Therefore there will be no feasible solution.
solution_html:
Linear constraints
{tex}x + y \geq 8{/tex}
{tex}3 x + 5 y \leq 15{/tex}
{tex}x \geq 0 , y \geq 0{/tex}
Objective function is min{tex} (Z) = 3x + 2y{/tex}
Reducing the all inequations into equations and finding their point of intersections, i.e.,
{tex}x + y = 8{/tex} ... (i)
{tex}3x + 5y = 15{/tex} ... (ii)
{tex}x = 0, y = 0{/tex} ... (iii)

Equations Point of Intersection
(i) and (ii) {tex}x = \frac { 25 } { 2 } \text { and } y = \frac { - 9 } { 2 }{/tex}
  {tex}\Rightarrow \left( \frac { 25 } { 2 } , \frac { - 9 } { 2 } \right){/tex}
(i) and (iii) when x = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = 8 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (0, 8)
  when y = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 8 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (8, 0)
(ii) and (iii) when x = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = 3 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (0, 3)
  when y = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 5 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (5, 0)

For feasible region.
For {tex}x + y \geq 8{/tex} let {tex}x = 0, y = 0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow 0 \geq 8{/tex} i.e., Not true
{tex}\Rightarrow {/tex} The shaded region will be away from origin
Again, for {tex}3 x + 5 y \leq 15{/tex}, let {tex}x = 0, y = 0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow 0 \leq 15{/tex} i.e. true
We have, no negative restriction, {tex}x \geq 0, y \geq 0{/tex} indicates that the shaded region will exist in first quadrant only.

The problem will not have any feasible region. Therefore there will be no feasible solution.

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Solve the Linear Programming Problem graphically: Maximise Z = 3x + 4y subject to the constraints: {tex}x + y \le4, \ x \geq 0, \ y \geq 0{/tex}
question_html:
Solve the Linear Programming Problem graphically:
Maximise Z = 3x + 4y subject to the constraints: {tex}x + y \le4, \ x \geq 0, \ y \geq 0{/tex}
solution_text: As {tex}x \geqslant 0,y \geqslant 0{/tex} , therefore we shall shade the other inequalities in the first quadrant only. Now {tex}x + y \leqslant 4{/tex} Let x + y = 4 {tex} \Rightarrow \frac{x}{4} + \frac{y}{4} = 1{/tex} Thus the line has 4 and 4 as intercepts along the axes. Now, (0, 0) satisfies the inequation, i.e., {tex}0 + 0 \leqslant 4{/tex} . Therefore, shaded region OAB is the feasible solution. Its corners are O (0, 0), A (4, 0), B (0, 4) At O (0, 0) Z = 0 At A (4, 0) Z = 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 4 = 12 At B (0, 4) Z = 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 4 = 16 Hence, max Z = 16 at x = 0, y = 4.
solution_html:
As {tex}x \geqslant 0,y \geqslant 0{/tex}, therefore we shall shade the other inequalities in the first quadrant only.
Now {tex}x + y \leqslant 4{/tex} 

Let x + y = 4
{tex} \Rightarrow \frac{x}{4} + \frac{y}{4} = 1{/tex}
Thus the line has 4 and 4 as intercepts along the axes. Now, (0, 0) satisfies the inequation, i.e., {tex}0 + 0 \leqslant 4{/tex}. Therefore, shaded region OAB is the feasible solution.
Its corners are O (0, 0), A (4, 0), B (0, 4)
At O (0, 0) Z = 0
At A (4, 0) Z = 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 4 = 12
At B (0, 4) Z = 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 4 = 16
Hence, max Z = 16 at x = 0, y = 4.

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Solve the following Linear Programming Problem graphically: Minimise z = -3x + 4y subject to the constraints x + 2y {tex}\leq{/tex} 8, 3x + 2y {tex}\leq{/tex} 12, x, y {tex}\geq{/tex} 0.
question_html:
Solve the following Linear Programming Problem graphically:
Minimise z = -3x + 4y
subject to the constraints
x + 2y {tex}\leq{/tex} 8,
3x + 2y {tex}\leq{/tex} 12,
x, y {tex}\geq{/tex} 0.
solution_text: Consider {tex}x + 2 y \leq 8{/tex} Let x + 2y = 8 {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { x } { 8 } + \frac { y } { 4 } = 1{/tex} {tex}\therefore {/tex} a = 8 , b = 4 Since, (0, 0) satisfies the inequation {tex}x + 2y \leqslant 8{/tex} Therefore, its solution contains (0, 0) Again {tex}3x + 2y \leqslant 12{/tex} Let 3x + 2y = 12 {tex} \Rightarrow \frac{x}{4} + \frac{y}{6} = 1{/tex} Again, (0, 0) satisfies {tex}3x + 2y \leqslant 2{/tex} Therefore its solution contains (0, 0). The feasible region is the solution set which is double shaded and is OABCO. At O (0, 0) Z = 0 At A (4, 0) Z = –3 {tex}\times{/tex} 4 = –12 At B (2, 3) Z = –3 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 + 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 3 = 6 At C (0, 4) Z = 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 4 = 16 Hence, minimum Z = –12 at x = 4, y = 0.
solution_html:
Consider  {tex}x + 2 y \leq 8{/tex} 
Let x + 2y = 8
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac { x } { 8 } + \frac { y } { 4 } = 1{/tex}
{tex}\therefore {/tex} a = 8 , b = 4

Since, (0, 0) satisfies the inequation {tex}x + 2y \leqslant 8{/tex}
Therefore, its solution contains (0, 0)
Again {tex}3x + 2y \leqslant 12{/tex}
Let 3x + 2y = 12
{tex} \Rightarrow \frac{x}{4} + \frac{y}{6} = 1{/tex}
Again, (0, 0) satisfies {tex}3x + 2y \leqslant 2{/tex}
Therefore its solution contains (0, 0).
The feasible region is the solution set which is double shaded and is OABCO.
At O (0, 0) Z = 0
At A (4, 0) Z = –3 {tex}\times{/tex} 4 = –12
At B (2, 3) Z = –3 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 + 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 3 = 6
At C (0, 4) Z = 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 4 = 16
Hence, minimum Z = –12 at x = 4, y = 0.

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Solve the Linear Programming Problem graphically: Maximize Z = 5x + 3y subject to the constraints 3x + 5y {tex}\leq{/tex} 15, 5x + 2y {tex}\leq{/tex} 10, x, y {tex}\geq{/tex} 0.
question_html:
Solve the Linear Programming Problem graphically:
Maximize Z = 5x + 3y
subject to the constraints
3x + 5y {tex}\leq{/tex} 15,
5x + 2y {tex}\leq{/tex} 10,
x, y {tex}\geq{/tex} 0.
solution_text: Converting the given inequations into equations, we get 3x + 5y = 15, 5x + 2y = 10, x = 0, y = 0 The region represented by 5x + 2y {tex}\leq{/tex} 10: The line meets coordinate axes at A 1 (2,0) and B 1 (0, 5)respectively. Join these points to obtain the line 5x + 2y = 10. Clearly, (0,0) satisfies the inequation 5x + 2y {tex}\leq{/tex} 10, so, the region in xy-plane that contains the origin represents the solution set of the given inequation. Region represented by 3x + 5y {tex}\leq{/tex} 15: The line meets coordinate axes at A 2 (5,0) and B 2 (0,3) respectively. Join these points to obtain the line 3x + 5y = 15. Clearly, (0,0) satisfies the inequation 3x + 5y {tex}\leq{/tex} 15, so, the region in xy -plane contains the origin represents the solution set of the given inequation. The region represented by x {tex}\geq {/tex} 0, y {tex}\geq {/tex} 0: It clearly represents the first quadrant of xy plane. Common region to regions represented by above inequalities is shown as shaded region. The coordinates of the corner points of the shaded region are O(0,0), A(2,0), P {tex}\left(\frac{20}{19}, \frac{45}{19}\right){/tex} , B 2 (0,3) The value of Z = 5x + 3y at O(0,0) = 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 0 + 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 0 = 0 A(2, 0) = 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 + 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 0 = 10 P {tex}\left(\frac{20}{19}, \frac{45}{19}\right){/tex} = 5 {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\frac{20}{19}{/tex} + 3 {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\frac{45}{19}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{235}{19}{/tex} B 2 (0,3) = 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 0 + 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 3 = 9 Clearly, z is maximum at P {tex}\left(\frac{20}{19}, \frac{45}{19}\right){/tex} So, x = {tex}\frac{20}{19}{/tex} , {tex}\frac{45}{19}{/tex} , and maximum value of Z = {tex}\frac{235}{19}{/tex}
solution_html:
Converting the given inequations into equations, we get
3x + 5y = 15, 5x + 2y = 10, x = 0, y = 0

The region represented by 5x + 2y {tex}\leq{/tex} 10:
The line meets coordinate axes at A1(2,0) and B1(0, 5)respectively. Join these points to obtain the line 5x + 2y = 10. 
Clearly, (0,0) satisfies the inequation 5x + 2y {tex}\leq{/tex} 10, so, the region in xy-plane that contains the origin represents the solution set of the given inequation. 
Region represented by 3x + 5y {tex}\leq{/tex} 15: 
The line meets coordinate axes at A2(5,0) and B2(0,3) respectively. Join these points to obtain the line 3x + 5y = 15. 
Clearly, (0,0) satisfies the inequation 3x + 5y {tex}\leq{/tex} 15, so, the region in xy -plane contains the origin represents the solution set of the given inequation.
The region represented by x {tex}\geq {/tex} 0, y {tex}\geq {/tex} 0: It clearly represents the first quadrant of xy plane.
Common region to regions represented by above inequalities is shown as shaded region. 
The coordinates of the corner points of the shaded region are O(0,0), A(2,0), P{tex}\left(\frac{20}{19}, \frac{45}{19}\right){/tex}, B2(0,3)
The value of Z = 5x + 3y at
O(0,0) = 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 0 + 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 0 = 0
A(2, 0) = 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 + 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 0 = 10
P{tex}\left(\frac{20}{19}, \frac{45}{19}\right){/tex} = 5 {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\frac{20}{19}{/tex} + 3 {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\frac{45}{19}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{235}{19}{/tex}
B2(0,3) = 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 0 + 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 3 = 9
Clearly, z is maximum at P{tex}\left(\frac{20}{19}, \frac{45}{19}\right){/tex}
So, x = {tex}\frac{20}{19}{/tex}, {tex}\frac{45}{19}{/tex}, and maximum value of Z = {tex}\frac{235}{19}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Solve the Linear Programming Problem graphically: Minimize Z = 3x + 5y such that {tex}x + 3y \geq 3,x + y \geq 2,x,y \geq 0{/tex} .
question_html:
Solve the Linear Programming Problem graphically:
Minimize Z = 3x + 5y such that {tex}x + 3y \geq 3,x + y \geq 2,x,y \geq 0{/tex}.
solution_text: For plotting the graphs of x + 3y = 3 and x + y = 2, we have the following tables: x 0 3 y 1 0 x 1 0 y 1 2 The feasible portion represented by the inequalities {tex}x + 3y \geqslant 3,x + y \geqslant 2{/tex} and {tex}x,y \geqslant 0{/tex} is represented by the shaded region , In the figure. The coordinates of point B are {tex}\left( {\frac{3}{2},\frac{1}{2}} \right){/tex} Which can be obtained by solving x + 3y = 3 and x + y = 2. At A(0, 2) {tex}Z = 3 \times 0 + 5 \times 2 = 10{/tex} At {tex}B\left( {\frac{3}{2},\frac{1}{2}} \right){/tex} {tex}Z = 3 \times \frac{3}{2} + 5 \times \frac{1}{2} = \frac{9}{2} + \frac{5}{2} = \frac{{14}}{2} = 7{/tex} At C (3, 0) {tex}Z = 3 \times 3 + 5 \times 0 = 9{/tex} Hence, Z is minimum is 7 when {tex}x = \frac{3}{2}{/tex} and {tex}y = \frac{1}{2}{/tex} .
solution_html:
For plotting the graphs of x + 3y = 3 and x + y = 2, we have the following tables:

x 0 3
y 1 0
x 1 0
y 1 2

The feasible portion represented by the inequalities
{tex}x + 3y \geqslant 3,x + y \geqslant 2{/tex} and {tex}x,y \geqslant 0{/tex} is represented by the  shaded region , 


In the figure. The coordinates of point B are {tex}\left( {\frac{3}{2},\frac{1}{2}} \right){/tex}
Which can be obtained by solving x + 3y = 3 and x + y = 2.
At A(0, 2)
{tex}Z = 3 \times 0 + 5 \times 2 = 10{/tex}
At {tex}B\left( {\frac{3}{2},\frac{1}{2}} \right){/tex}
{tex}Z = 3 \times \frac{3}{2} + 5 \times \frac{1}{2} = \frac{9}{2} + \frac{5}{2} = \frac{{14}}{2} = 7{/tex}
At C (3, 0)
{tex}Z = 3 \times 3 + 5 \times 0 = 9{/tex}

Hence, Z is minimum is 7 when {tex}x = \frac{3}{2}{/tex} and {tex}y = \frac{1}{2}{/tex}.

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Solve the Linear Programming Problem graphically: Maximize Z = 3x + 2y subject to {tex}x + 2y \leq 10,3x + y \leq 15,x,y \geq0{/tex}
question_html:
Solve the Linear Programming Problem graphically:
Maximize Z = 3x + 2y subject to {tex}x + 2y \leq 10,3x + y \leq 15,x,y \geq0{/tex}
solution_text: Consider {tex}x + 2y \leqslant 10{/tex} Let x + 2y = 10 {tex} \Rightarrow \frac{x}{{10}} + \frac{y}{5} = 1{/tex} Since, (0, 0) satisfies the inequation, therefore the half plane containing (0, 0) is the required plane. Again {tex}3x + 2y \leqslant 15{/tex} Let 3x + y = 15 {tex} \Rightarrow \frac{x}{5} + \frac{y}{{15}} = 1{/tex} It also satisfies by (0, 0) and its required half plane contains (0, 0). Now double shaded region in the first quadrant contains the solution. Now OABC represents the feasible region. Z = 3x + 2y At O (0, 0) Z = 3 {tex} \times {/tex} 0 + 2 {tex} \times {/tex} 0 = 0 At A (5, 0) Z = 3 {tex} \times {/tex} 5 + 2 {tex} \times {/tex} 0 = 15 At B (4, 3) Z = 3 {tex} \times {/tex} 4 + 2 {tex} \times {/tex} 3 = 18 At C (0, 5) Z = 3 {tex} \times {/tex} 0 + 2 {tex} \times {/tex} 5 = 10 Hence, Z is maximum i.e., 18 at x = 4, y = 3.
solution_html:
Consider {tex}x + 2y \leqslant 10{/tex}
Let x + 2y = 10
{tex} \Rightarrow \frac{x}{{10}} + \frac{y}{5} = 1{/tex}


Since, (0, 0) satisfies the inequation, therefore the half plane containing (0, 0) is the required plane.
Again {tex}3x + 2y \leqslant 15{/tex}
Let 3x + y = 15
{tex} \Rightarrow \frac{x}{5} + \frac{y}{{15}} = 1{/tex}
It also satisfies by (0, 0) and its required half plane contains (0, 0).
Now double shaded region in the first quadrant contains the solution.
Now OABC represents the feasible region.
Z = 3x + 2y
At  O (0, 0) Z = 3 {tex} \times {/tex} 0 + 2 {tex} \times {/tex} 0 = 0
At  A (5, 0) Z = 3 {tex} \times {/tex} 5 + 2 {tex} \times {/tex} 0 = 15
At  B (4, 3) Z = 3 {tex} \times {/tex} 4 + 2 {tex} \times {/tex} 3 = 18
At  C (0, 5) Z = 3 {tex} \times {/tex} 0 + 2 {tex} \times {/tex} 5 = 10

Hence, Z is maximum i.e., 18 at x = 4, y = 3.

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Solve the Linear Programming Problem graphically: Minimise Z = x + 2y subject to 2x + y {tex}\ge{/tex} 3, x + 2y {tex}\ge{/tex} 6, x, y {tex}\ge{/tex} 0.
question_html:
Solve the Linear Programming Problem graphically: 
Minimise Z = x + 2y subject to 2x + y {tex}\ge{/tex} 3, x + 2y {tex}\ge{/tex} 6, x, y {tex}\ge{/tex} 0.
solution_text: Objective function is: Z = x + 2y Constraints are: {tex}2 \mathrm{x}+\mathrm{y} \geq 3, \\ \mathrm{x}+2 \mathrm{y} \geq 6, \\\mathrm{x}, \mathrm{y} \geq 0{/tex} Now for {tex}x+2 y \geq 6{/tex} we have x 0 6 y 3 0 (x, y) = (0, 3), (6, 0) For {tex}2 x+y \geq 3{/tex} we have x 0 1 y 3 1 (x, y) = (0, 3), (1, 1) Corner Points Value of Z (0, 3) 6 (6, 0) 6 As per the table minimum value of Z is 6 but we can see that the feasible region is unbounded. Thus, 6 may or may not be the minimum value of Z {tex}\therefore{/tex} We will plot the graph of inequality, x + 2y < 6 Here we will see that there is no common point with the feasible region. Hence, Z will be minimum on all points joining line (0, 3), (6, 0). Therefore, Z will be minimum on x + 2y = 6
solution_html:
Objective function is:
Z = x + 2y
Constraints are:
{tex}2 \mathrm{x}+\mathrm{y} \geq 3, \\ \mathrm{x}+2 \mathrm{y} \geq 6, \\\mathrm{x}, \mathrm{y} \geq 0{/tex} 
Now for {tex}x+2 y \geq 6{/tex} we have

x 0 6
y 3 0

(x, y) = (0, 3), (6, 0)
For {tex}2 x+y \geq 3{/tex} we have

x 0 1
y 3 1

(x, y) = (0, 3), (1, 1)

Corner Points Value of Z
(0, 3) 6
(6, 0) 6

As per the table minimum value of Z is 6 but we can see that the feasible region is unbounded. Thus, 6 may or may not be the minimum value of Z
{tex}\therefore{/tex} We will plot the graph of inequality, x + 2y < 6
Here we will see that there is no common point with the feasible region.
Hence, Z will be minimum on all points joining line (0, 3), (6, 0). Therefore, Z will be minimum on x + 2y = 6

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Show that the minimum of Z occurs at more than two points. Minimize and Maximize Z = 5x + 10y subject to {tex}x + 2y \leq 120, \ x + y \geq 60{/tex} , {tex}x - 2y \geq 0, \ x, \ y \geq 0{/tex} .
question_html:
Show that the minimum of Z occurs at more than two points.
Minimize and Maximize Z = 5x + 10y subject to {tex}x + 2y \leq 120, \ x + y \geq 60{/tex}, {tex}x - 2y \geq 0, \ x, \ y \geq 0{/tex}.
solution_text: Consider {tex}x + 2y \leqslant 120{/tex} Let x + 2y = 120 {tex} \Rightarrow \frac{x}{{120}} + \frac{y}{{60}} = 1{/tex} The half plane containing(0, 0) is the required half plane as (0, 0) makes {tex}x + 2y \leqslant 120{/tex} , true. x 0 30 60 y 60 45 30 Again {tex}x + y \geqslant 60{/tex} Let x + y = 60 Also the half plane containing (0, 0) does not make {tex}x + y \geqslant 60{/tex} true. Therefore, the required half plane does not contain (0, 0). Again {tex}x - 2y \geqslant 0{/tex} Let x - 2y = 0 {tex} \Rightarrow x = 2y{/tex} Let test point be (30, 0). x 0 30 60 y 0 15 30 {tex} \Rightarrow x - 2y \geqslant 0 \Rightarrow 30 - 2 \times 0 \geqslant 0{/tex} It is true. Therefore, the half plane contains (30, 0). The region CFEKC represents the feasible region. At C (60, 0) Z {tex} = 5 \times 60 = 300{/tex} At F (120, 0) {tex}Z = 5 \times 120 = 600{/tex} At E (60, 30) {tex}Z = 5 \times 60 + 10 \times 30 = 600{/tex} At K (40, 20) {tex}Z = 5 \times 40 + 10 \times 20 = 400{/tex} Hence, minimum Z = 300 at x = 60, y = 0 and maximum Z = 600 at x = 120, y = 0 or x = 60, y = 30.
solution_html:
Consider {tex}x + 2y \leqslant 120{/tex}
Let x + 2y = 120
{tex} \Rightarrow \frac{x}{{120}} + \frac{y}{{60}} = 1{/tex}
The half plane containing(0, 0) is the required half plane as (0, 0) makes {tex}x + 2y \leqslant 120{/tex}, true.

x 0 30 60
y 60 45 30

Again {tex}x + y \geqslant 60{/tex}
Let x + y = 60
Also the half plane containing (0, 0) does not make {tex}x + y \geqslant 60{/tex} true.
Therefore, the required half plane does not contain (0, 0).
Again {tex}x - 2y \geqslant 0{/tex}
Let x - 2y = 0 {tex} \Rightarrow x = 2y{/tex}
Let test point be (30, 0).

x 0 30 60
y 0 15 30

{tex} \Rightarrow x - 2y \geqslant 0 \Rightarrow 30 - 2 \times 0 \geqslant 0{/tex} It is true.
Therefore, the half plane contains (30, 0).
The region CFEKC represents the feasible region.
At C (60, 0) Z {tex} = 5 \times 60 = 300{/tex}
At F (120, 0) {tex}Z = 5 \times 120 = 600{/tex}
At E (60, 30){tex}Z = 5 \times 60 + 10 \times 30 = 600{/tex}
At K (40, 20) {tex}Z = 5 \times 40 + 10 \times 20 = 400{/tex}
Hence, minimum Z = 300 at x = 60, y = 0 and maximum Z = 600 at x = 120, y = 0 or x = 60, y = 30.

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Show that the minimum of Z occurs at more than two points. Minimise and Maximise Z = x + 2y subject to {tex}x + 2 y \geq 100,2 x - y \leq 0,2 x + y \leq 200{/tex} ; {tex}x , \ y \geq 0{/tex} .
question_html:
Show that the minimum of Z occurs at more than two points.
Minimise and Maximise Z = x + 2y subject to {tex}x + 2 y \geq 100,2 x - y \leq 0,2 x + y \leq 200{/tex}; {tex}x , \ y \geq 0{/tex}.
solution_text: We have linear constraints as {tex}x + 2 y \geq 100{/tex} {tex}2 x - y \leq 0{/tex} {tex}2 x + y \leq 200{/tex} {tex}x \geq 0 , y \geq 0{/tex} and objective function is min or max {tex}(Z) = x + 2y{/tex} Now, reducing the above inequations into equations and finding their point of intersections i.e., {tex}x + 2y = 100{/tex} ...(i) {tex}2x - y = 0{/tex} ...(ii) {tex}2x + y = 200{/tex} ...(iii) {tex}x = 0, y = 0 {/tex} ...(iv) Equations Point of Intersection (i) and (ii) {tex}x = 20{/tex} and {tex}y = 40{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} point is {tex} (20, 40){/tex} (i) and (iii) {tex}x = 100{/tex} and {tex}y = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} point is {tex}(100, 0){/tex} (i) and (iv) when {tex} x = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}y = 50{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} point is {tex}(0, 50){/tex} when {tex}y = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}x = 100{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} point {tex}(100, 0){/tex} (i) and (iii) {tex}x = 50, y = 100{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} point is {tex}(50, 100){/tex} (i) and (iv) {tex}x = 0 , y = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} point is {tex}(0, 0){/tex} (i) and (iv) when x = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = 200 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} point is (0, 200) when {tex}y = 0 , x = 100{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} point is {tex}(100, 0){/tex} Now for feasible region, using origin testing method for each constraint For {tex}x + 2 y \geq 100{/tex} , let {tex}x = 0, y = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 0 \geq 100{/tex} i.e., true {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} the shaded region will be away from the origin For {tex}2 x + y \leq 200{/tex} , let {tex}x = 0, y = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 0 \leq 200{/tex} i.e., true {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} The shaded region will be toward the origin. Also, non-negative restrictions {tex}x \geq 0 , y \geq 0{/tex} indicates that the feasible region will be exist in first quadrant. Now, corner points are {tex}A(0, 50), B(20, 40), C(50, 100)\ and\ D(0, 200){/tex} For optimal solution substituting the value of all comer points in {tex}Z = x + 2y{/tex} Corner points {tex}Z = x + 2y{/tex} {tex}A (0, 50){/tex} {tex}\mathrm { Z } = 0 + 2 \times 50 = 100{/tex} Minimum {tex}B (20, 40){/tex} {tex}\mathrm { Z } = 20 + 2 \times 40 = 100{/tex} {tex}C (50, 100){/tex} {tex}Z = 50 + 2 \times 100 = 250{/tex} {tex}D (0, 200){/tex} {tex}Z = 0 + 2 \times 200 = 400{/tex} {tex}\to{/tex} Maximum Hence, minimum (Z) = 100 at all points on the line segment joining the points A(0, 50) and B(20, 40) : Maximum (Z) = 400 at (0, 200)
solution_html:
We have linear constraints as
{tex}x + 2 y \geq 100{/tex}
{tex}2 x - y \leq 0{/tex}
{tex}2 x + y \leq 200{/tex}
{tex}x \geq 0 , y \geq 0{/tex}
and objective function is min or max {tex}(Z) = x + 2y{/tex}
Now, reducing the above inequations into equations and finding their point of intersections i.e.,
{tex}x + 2y = 100{/tex} ...(i)
{tex}2x - y = 0{/tex} ...(ii)
{tex}2x + y = 200{/tex} ...(iii)
{tex}x = 0, y = 0 {/tex}...(iv)

Equations Point of Intersection
(i) and (ii) {tex}x = 20{/tex} and {tex}y = 40{/tex}
  {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} point is{tex} (20, 40){/tex}
(i) and (iii) {tex}x = 100{/tex} and {tex}y = 0{/tex}
  {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} point is {tex}(100, 0){/tex}
(i) and (iv) when{tex} x = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}y = 50{/tex}
  {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} point is {tex}(0, 50){/tex}
  when {tex}y = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}x = 100{/tex}
  {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} point {tex}(100, 0){/tex}
(i) and (iii) {tex}x = 50, y = 100{/tex}
  {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} point is {tex}(50, 100){/tex}
(i) and (iv) {tex}x = 0 , y = 0{/tex}
  {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} point is {tex}(0, 0){/tex}
(i) and (iv) when x = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = 200
  {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} point is (0, 200)
  when {tex}y = 0 , x = 100{/tex}
  {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} point is {tex}(100, 0){/tex}

Now for feasible region, using origin testing method for each constraint
For {tex}x + 2 y \geq 100{/tex}, let {tex}x = 0, y = 0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow 0 \geq 100{/tex} i.e., true {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} the shaded region will be away from the origin
For {tex}2 x + y \leq 200{/tex}, let {tex}x = 0, y = 0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow 0 \leq 200{/tex} i.e., true {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} The shaded region will be toward the origin.
Also, non-negative restrictions {tex}x \geq 0 , y \geq 0{/tex} indicates that the feasible region will be exist in first quadrant.
Now, corner points are {tex}A(0, 50), B(20, 40), C(50, 100)\ and\ D(0, 200){/tex}

For optimal solution substituting the value of all comer points in {tex}Z = x + 2y{/tex}

Corner points {tex}Z = x + 2y{/tex}
{tex}A (0, 50){/tex} {tex}\mathrm { Z } = 0 + 2 \times 50 = 100{/tex} Minimum
{tex}B (20, 40){/tex} {tex}\mathrm { Z } = 20 + 2 \times 40 = 100{/tex}
{tex}C (50, 100){/tex} {tex}Z = 50 + 2 \times 100 = 250{/tex}  
{tex}D (0, 200){/tex} {tex}Z = 0 + 2 \times 200 = 400{/tex} {tex}\to{/tex} Maximum

Hence, minimum (Z) = 100 at all points on the line segment joining the points A(0, 50) and B(20, 40) : Maximum (Z) = 400 at (0, 200)

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Show that the minimum of Z occurs at more than two points. Maximize Z = -x + 2y subject to the constraints: {tex}x \geq 3,x + y \geq 5,x + 2y \geq 6,y \geq 0{/tex} .
question_html:
Show that the minimum of Z occurs at more than two points.
Maximize Z = -x + 2y subject to the constraints: {tex}x \geq 3,x + y \geq 5,x + 2y \geq 6,y \geq 0{/tex}.
solution_text: Consider {tex}x \geqslant 3{/tex} Let x = 3 which is a line parallel to y-axis at a positive distance of 3 from it. Since {tex}x \geqslant 3{/tex} , therefore the required half-plane does not contain (0, 0). Now consider {tex}x + y \geqslant 5{/tex} Let x + y = 5 {tex} \Rightarrow \frac{x}{5} + \frac{y}{5} = 1{/tex} Now (0, 0) does not satisfy {tex}x + y \geqslant 5{/tex} , therefore the required half plane does not contain (0, 0). Again consider {tex}x + 2y \geqslant 6{/tex} . Let x + 2y = 6 {tex} \Rightarrow \frac{x}{6} + \frac{y}{3} = 1{/tex} Here also (0, 0) does not satisfy {tex}x + 2y \geqslant6{/tex} , therefore the required half plane does not contain (0, 0). The corners of the feasible region are A (6, 0), B (4, 1) and C (3, 2). At A (6, 0) Z = –6 + 2 {tex} \times {/tex} 0 = –6 At B (4, 1) Z = –4 + 2 {tex} \times {/tex} 1 = –2 At C (3, 2) Z = –3 + 2 {tex} \times {/tex} 2 = 1 Hence, maximum Z = 1 at x = 3, y = 2.
solution_html:
Consider {tex}x \geqslant 3{/tex}
Let x = 3 which is a line parallel to y-axis at a positive distance of 3 from it.

Since {tex}x \geqslant 3{/tex}, therefore the required half-plane does not contain (0, 0).

Now consider {tex}x + y \geqslant 5{/tex}
Let x + y = 5
{tex} \Rightarrow \frac{x}{5} + \frac{y}{5} = 1{/tex}
Now (0, 0) does not satisfy {tex}x + y \geqslant 5{/tex}, therefore the required half plane does not contain (0, 0).
Again consider {tex}x + 2y \geqslant 6{/tex}.
Let x + 2y = 6
{tex} \Rightarrow \frac{x}{6} + \frac{y}{3} = 1{/tex}
Here also (0, 0) does not satisfy {tex}x + 2y \geqslant6{/tex}, therefore the required half plane does not contain (0, 0).
The corners of the feasible region are A (6, 0), B (4, 1) and C (3, 2).
At  A (6, 0)  Z = –6  + 2 {tex} \times {/tex} 0 = –6
At  B (4, 1)  Z = –4 + 2 {tex} \times {/tex} 1 = –2
At  C (3, 2)  Z = –3 + 2 {tex} \times {/tex} 2 = 1
Hence, maximum Z = 1 at x = 3, y = 2.

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Show that the minimum of Z occurs at more than two points. Maximize Z = x + y, subject to {tex}x - y \leq - 1, - x + y \leq 0, \ x, \ y \geq 0{/tex} .
question_html:
Show that the minimum of Z occurs at more than two points.
Maximize Z = x + y, subject to {tex}x - y \leq - 1, - x + y \leq 0, \ x, \ y \geq 0{/tex}.
solution_text: Consider {tex}x - y \leqslant - 1{/tex} Let x - y = -1 {tex} \Rightarrow x = y - 1{/tex} A B C D x -1 0 2 3 y 0 1 2 4 If (0, 0) is the test point then {tex}x - y \leqslant - 1 \Rightarrow 0 \leqslant - 1{/tex} which is false and thus the required plane does not include (0, 0). Again {tex} - x + y \leqslant 0{/tex} Let -x + y = 0 {tex} \Rightarrow y = x{/tex} O E F x 0 1 2 y 0 1 2 For (1, 0) – 1 {tex} \leqslant {/tex} 0 which is true, therefore the required half-plane include (1, 0). It is clear that the two required half planes do not intersect at all, i.e., they do not have a common region. Hence there is no maximum Z.
solution_html:
Consider {tex}x - y \leqslant - 1{/tex}
Let x - y = -1
{tex} \Rightarrow x = y - 1{/tex}

  A B C D
x -1 0 2 3
y 0 1 2 4

If (0, 0) is the test point then {tex}x - y \leqslant - 1 \Rightarrow 0 \leqslant - 1{/tex} which is false and thus the required plane does not include (0, 0).
Again {tex} - x + y \leqslant 0{/tex}
Let -x + y = 0
{tex} \Rightarrow y = x{/tex}

  O E F
x 0 1 2

For (1, 0) – 1 {tex} \leqslant {/tex}0 which is true, therefore the required half-plane include (1, 0).
